title: The Five Constraints
description: Why each constraint exists, what breaks when it fails, and how both case studies satisfy them.

stops.title: Start with the Pre-filter
stops.narrative:
Before running the five constraints, the [three-question pre-filter](node:three-question-test) screens for basic blockchain fit. Multi-party data? Institutional anchor? Verifiable history?

Both case studies pass all three. The [Battery Regulation](node:battery-regulation) has manufacturers, consumers, and authorities sharing product data, with the European Commission as anchor. [GDPR](node:gdpr) has controllers, subjects, and SAs sharing compliance evidence, with supervisory authorities as anchor. If only 1-2 hold, stop here — use a database.

stops.title: 1. Data Cadence
stops.narrative:
[Data cadence](node:constraint-data-cadence): the regulation's update rhythm must fit L1 settlement. No real-time streaming at 100ms intervals.

Battery SoH readings: monthly at most. GDPR consent events: sporadic. [Breach notifications](node:gdpr-breach-notification): rare, 72h window. [Rights requests](node:gdpr-rights-requests): 1-month window. Both are event-driven or periodic — perfectly compatible.

The [MPT-per-operator](node:mpt-per-operator) pattern decouples item volume from on-chain rate. 10,000 items in one batch transaction.

stops.title: 2. Sequential Access
stops.narrative:
[Sequential access](node:constraint-sequential-access): no concurrent contention on the same data structure. The MPT in a single UTxO is inherently sequential — one transaction per block.

This is a feature, not a limitation, when the regulation serializes access. Batteries: one manufacturer per trie. GDPR: one [controller](node:gdpr-controller) per compliance trie. [Relay patterns](node:relay-state-machine) (subject to controller to SA) follow the regulation's own ordering.

stops.title: 3. Liveness
stops.narrative:
[Liveness](node:constraint-liveness): if one actor stops, the chain stalls. The regulation must provide deadlines and penalties as external incentive.

Battery Regulation: Member State penalties under Art. 93, plus market restriction or withdrawal measures. GDPR: [fines up to 4% global turnover](node:gdpr-fines), the strongest penalties of any regulation analysed. The 72-hour and 1-month deadlines are themselves liveness mechanisms — they impose time pressure that prevents indefinite stalling.

Protocol escalation (slot-based timeouts, bond slashing) can supplement regulatory penalties.

stops.title: 4. Fee Alignment
stops.narrative:
[Fee alignment](node:constraint-fee-alignment): someone must benefit enough from on-chain compliance to pay. The [sponsor-pays](node:sponsor-pays) principle from the thesis.

Battery manufacturers: compliance costs ~$0.10-0.15 per reading vs fines and market withdrawal. GDPR [controllers](node:gdpr-controller): ~50-100 EUR/year vs median fines of 50,000+ EUR. In both cases, the obligated party pays because compliance is cheaper than non-compliance. The [user](node:user) never pays — no wallet, no ADA, no friction.

stops.title: 5. Identity Delegation
stops.narrative:
[Identity delegation](node:constraint-identity-delegation): non-crypto actors must make meaningful on-chain state transitions via cryptographic proxies.

Batteries use [physical mode](node:physical-mode): NFC secure element in the product. The consumer taps a phone — the SE050 signs — no wallet needed. GDPR uses [process mode](node:process-mode): the [data subject](node:gdpr-data-subject) interacts through the controller's web portal. The authenticated session IS the [signing function](node:signing-function).

Different substrates, same principle: the user never sees the blockchain.

stops.title: Everything Connects
stops.narrative:
The five constraints are not independent — they reinforce each other through the architecture.

[MPT-per-operator](node:mpt-per-operator) satisfies both [data cadence](node:constraint-data-cadence) and [sequential access](node:constraint-sequential-access). The [signing function](node:signing-function) implements [identity delegation](node:constraint-identity-delegation). [Fines](node:gdpr-fines) and penalties provide [liveness](node:constraint-liveness) and justify [fee alignment](node:constraint-fee-alignment).

When all five hold, the regulation and the architecture are working together rather than fighting each other.
